g.data-narration: Welcome to our lecture on Operating Systems. Today we'll explore what OS does and why it matters.
An Operating System is the fundamental software that acts as an intermediary between users and computer hardware, managing all system resources.
g.text: An OS is the most fundamental software that runs on a computer.
It acts as an intermediary between the user and the computer
hardware, providing an efficient environment for program execution.

g.data-narration: An operating system serves two fundamental roles: as an extended machine that hides hardware complexity, and as a resource manager that allocates resources.
Process Management handles CPU scheduling, creating and terminating processes, and enabling communication between processes through IPC mechanisms.
g.text: runs on CPU for how long
Multitasking: OS creates illusion of parallel execution

g.data-narration: Memory Management allocates and deallocates memory, provides protection between processes, and implements virtual memory to run programs larger than physical RAM.
File System Management provides a uniform logical view of storage, handling file operations, directory organization, access control, and disk scheduling.
Device Management handles I/O through drivers, uses buffering and caching for speed differences, and implements spooling for devices like printers.
The OS as an extended machine provides abstractions like files and system calls, hiding the complex bare metal details from programmers.
g.text: Complex: Direct hardware control, tracks and sectors
System Calls: The gateway between applications and OS services

g.data-narration: As a resource manager, the OS allocates CPU cycles, memory, and I/O fairly while preventing conflicts and ensuring system stability.
g.text: Key: Allocation policies must balance efficiency and fairness

g.data-narration: Operating systems provide essential services including user interface, program execution, I/O operations, file system access, communications, and error detection.
In summary, the OS performs four core functions: process, memory, file, and device management while serving as both an extended machine and resource manager.
g.text: Dual Role: Extended Machine + Resource Manager
